::: {.proof}

Adapted from [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1, p. 257].
This proof assembles the seven lemmas @lem-censored-lik, @lem-surv-discretized, @lem-lik-by-interval, @lem-ph-single-subj-fail, @lem-ph-whether-failure, @lem-ph-event-chain-rule, and @lem-ph-which-subject.

Assume noninformative censoring
(conditional on $\vx$, censoring times are independent of event times)
and no tied event times.
Let $t_1 < \cdots < t_K$ be the distinct observed failure times,
and let $(i)$ be the subject who fails at $t_i$ (unique under the no-ties assumption).

By @lem-lik-by-interval the likelihood factors over intervals,
$\Lik \approx \prod_\tau \Lik_\tau$.
Separate the $K$ intervals that contain a failure from the rest.
A no-failure interval contributes
$\Lik_\tau^{\text{no failure}} = \prod_{m \in R(\tau)}\paren{1 - q_m(\tau)}$;
the interval containing $t_i$ contributes the event factor
$\Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}$ times the survival of the other members,
which to first order is just the event factor itself, since each other member survives with probability $1 - O(\Delta t)$.
Applying @lem-ph-event-chain-rule to each event factor,
and then @lem-ph-whether-failure and @lem-ph-which-subject to its two pieces,

$$
\ba
\Lik
&\approx
\paren{\prod_{i=1}^{K}
   \Pr\sb{(i) \text{ fails} \mid 1 \text{ failure at } t_i,\ R(t_i)}\,
   \Pr\sb{1 \text{ failure at } t_i \mid R(t_i)}}
   \paren{\prod_{\tau\,:\,\text{no failure}} \Lik_\tau^{\text{no failure}}}
&& \text{(@lem-ph-event-chain-rule)}
\\
&\approx
\underbrace{\paren{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \frac{\red{\hazfactor(\vx_{(i)})}}{\red{S_i}}}}
          _{\Lik^*(\vb)\ \text{(kept)}}
\;\times\;
\underbrace{
\sb{\prod_{i=1}^{K} \dblue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}\;\red{S_i}}
\sb{\prod_{\tau\,:\,\text{no failure}} \prod_{m \in R(\tau)} \paren{1 - q_m(\tau)}}}
          _{\mathcal{B}(\vb,\,\haz_0)\ \text{(discarded)}} .
&& \text{(@lem-ph-whether-failure, @lem-ph-which-subject)}
\ea
$$ {#eq-ph-factored-lik}

Under the no-ties assumption each event time has $d_i = 1$
(where $d_i$ is the number of events at $t_i$),
so the kept product $\prod_{i=1}^{K}(\cdots) = \prod_{\set{i:\ d_i = 1}}(\cdots)$
matches the form of $\Lik^*(\vb)$ in @def-ph-partial-lik.

The partial likelihood keeps the first product, $\Lik^*(\vb)$,
and drops the second, $\mathcal{B}(\vb, \haz_0)$.
The discarded factor $\mathcal{B}(\vb, \haz_0)$ collects both the
whether-a-failure-occurs factors and the entire between-event survival background,
and it is the only place the baseline hazard $\dblue{\haz_0(\cdot)}$ appears —
equivalently, the shared $\dblue{\haz_0(t_i)\,\Delta t}$ cancels from each kept factor
in @eq-ph-which-subject — which is why maximizing $\Lik^*(\vb)$ alone
frees the estimator from $\haz_0(\cdot)$.
The discarded factor is **not** free of $\vb$, however:
it still depends on $\vb$ through the risk-set score totals $\red{S_i}$ and the survival background.
Dropping it therefore sacrifices the information about $\vb$ carried by
*when* (and within how large a risk set) failures occur,
retaining only the information in *which* subject fails at each event time.

The factorization in @eq-ph-factored-lik is a *construction* rather than
an exact decomposition of the joint probability:
the factors are not formally independent
(the risk set $R(t_{i+1})$ depends on who failed and was censored before $t_{i+1}$,
and the discretization is exact only in the $\Delta t \to 0$ limit),
but Cox showed that treating $\Lik^*(\vb)$ as a likelihood — i.e.,
maximizing it as a function of $\vb$ — yields estimators with the
usual large-sample properties
(consistency, asymptotic normality, the standard score and information identities;
see also [@klein2003survival, §8.3, Theoretical Note 1]).
Under noninformative censoring, each kept factor depends on the observed history
only through $R(t_i)$ and $\set{\vx_j}_{j \in R(t_i)}$,
and @eq-ph-which-subject gives exactly the partial likelihood $\Lik^*(\vb)$ of @def-ph-partial-lik.

:::
